q: What does this print?

opts.why: Both operands are int, so / is integer division. The double conversion happens after.

opts.t: Compile error
opts.why: Assigning int division result to double is valid; the truncation is silent.

why: a/b is int/int so the compiler performs integer division, yielding 3. The result is then widened to double 3.0 and printed as 3. To get 3.5, cast first: (double)a / b or a / 2.0.
fj: "Assigning to double means the division is floating-point."
fs: "Integer division truncates before any widening — cast at least one operand first."

q: Is there a bug? If so, what?

opts.t: Out-of-bounds write when i==5; UB
No bug; arr[5] is the last valid slot
opts.why: arr has indices 0-4. Index 5 is one past the end — writing it is undefined behaviour.

opts.t: Compile error: array index type mismatch
opts.why: int index into int[] compiles fine; the error is purely runtime UB.

why: The loop condition is i <= 5, so i reaches 5 and arr[5] is written. C arrays have no bounds checking; this is undefined behaviour and can corrupt the stack. Fix: i < 5.
fj: "Arrays of size N go from 0 to N, inclusive."
fs: "Off-by-one on array bounds is silent UB — always use i < N, or prefer std::array with at()."

q: What is the bug in this condition?

opts.t: = assigns 5 to x; condition is always true
Compile error: assignment in condition
opts.why: It compiles (maybe with a warning). C++ allows assignment inside if; the value of the expression is 5 which is truthy.

opts.t: Prints nothing; x remains 0
opts.why: The assignment sets x to 5 and the condition evaluates to 5 (non-zero), so the block runs.

why: x = 5 is an assignment expression with value 5. Since 5 != 0, the condition is always true regardless of x's original value. The intent was x == 5. Modern compilers warn with -Wall; treat this warning as an error.
fj: "= and == both test equality inside if."
fs: "Enable -Wparentheses/-Wall; the Yoda idiom (5 == x) also catches this at compile time."

q: Which line throws and which is safe?

opts.t: A is silent UB; B throws std::out_of_range
Both lines throw std::out_of_range
opts.why: operator[] never throws — it performs no bounds check and invokes UB on bad index.

opts.t: A throws; B is silent UB
opts.why: It is exactly the opposite: [] is unchecked, at() is checked and throws.

why: std::vector::operator[] does NOT bounds-check; out-of-range access is undefined behaviour. std::vector::at() bounds-checks and throws std::out_of_range. In debug code prefer at(); in hot paths where bounds are already verified, [] is fine.
fj: "vector[] and vector.at() are interchangeable."
fs: "Use at() when you want checked access; [] in performance-critical code with known-good indices."

q: What happens to the iterator after push_back?

opts.t: Undefined behaviour — iterator is invalidated
Prints 1 — iterator still valid
opts.why: push_back may reallocate the internal buffer, invalidating ALL iterators, pointers, and references.

opts.t: Compile error: iterator used after modification
opts.why: The compiler cannot detect iterator invalidation at compile time; it's a runtime UB.

why: If push_back triggers a reallocation (capacity exceeded), the vector moves its storage to a new address and all iterators into the old buffer are dangling. If no reallocation occurs, only end() is invalidated and all other iterators remain valid — but because you cannot predict reallocation at runtime without an explicit reserve(), treat any mutation as potentially invalidating. Cache the index instead of the iterator if you must mutate.
fj: "Iterators into a vector are always stable across push_back as long as size stays within capacity."
fs: "Never use iterators or pointers into a vector across any mutating operation unless you reserved ahead."

q: What is the object-slicing bug here?
lines: struct Base { virtual void f(); int x; };

opts.t: d is sliced — only Base part copied; y lost
Derived is passed correctly via implicit conversion
opts.why: Base b is a value parameter. The Derived object is copied into a Base, truncating Derived members.

opts.t: Compile error: cannot pass Derived to Base parameter
opts.why: Derived is a subtype of Base; the call compiles. The slicing is silent and dangerous.

why: Passing by value copies only the Base sub-object. The Derived portion (y) is discarded. Worse, virtual dispatch is lost — f() resolves statically to Base::f(). Pass by pointer or reference to preserve polymorphism.
fj: "C++ automatically handles subtype polymorphism by value."
fs: "Always pass polymorphic objects by pointer or reference; value parameters slice."

q: What is wrong with returning a reference here?

opts.t: Dangling reference — tmp destroyed on return
Fine — const reference extends tmp's lifetime
opts.why: Binding a const reference to a local extends its life only within the same scope, not across function calls.

opts.t: Compile error: cannot return reference to local
opts.why: Most compilers warn (-Wreturn-local-addr) but still compile it; using the result is UB.

why: tmp is a local variable. It is destroyed when getName() returns. The returned const std::string& is a dangling reference; any use of it is undefined behaviour. Return by value instead.
fj: "const & extends the lifetime of the object being referenced."
fs: "Returning a reference to a local is always UB; return by value and let NRVO optimize it."

q: Why does this member function have unexpected behaviour?

opts.t: Parameter shadows member; member is unchanged
Increments the member count correctly
opts.why: The parameter count shadows the member count. count++ modifies the local parameter, not the member.

opts.t: Compile error: redefinition of count
opts.why: C++ allows shadowing; the compiler warns with -Wshadow but accepts it.

why: The parameter int count shadows the member variable count. Inside the function, count refers to the parameter. Use this->count++ to explicitly reference the member, or rename either the parameter or the member to avoid ambiguity.
fj: "count++ inside a member function always modifies the member."
fs: "Enable -Wshadow; use this-> when parameter names collide with member names."

q: This class manages a raw pointer. What rule is violated?

opts.t: Rule of Three violated — shallow copy causes double-free
Destructor leaks memory
opts.why: delete[] data is correct. The leak would occur if there was no destructor, but here it exists.

opts.t: No bug — compiler-generated copy is safe
opts.why: The compiler-generated copy constructor does a shallow copy of the pointer, leading to two objects pointing to the same memory; both will delete[] it.

why: Rule of Three: if you define a destructor, you must also define copy constructor and copy assignment operator. The compiler-generated copies do a bitwise copy of the pointer — two Buffer objects sharing the same data pointer will both delete[] it, causing undefined behaviour. Use Rule of Five or prefer std::vector.
fj: "The compiler-generated copy is fine when a destructor is defined."
fs: "Resource-owning classes need destructor + copy ctor + copy assign (Rule of Three) or move semantics (Rule of Five)."

q: Is this string_view usage safe?

opts.t: Dangling string_view — s is destroyed after return
Safe — string_view is a value type that copies the content
opts.why: string_view is a non-owning view (pointer+length). It does not copy the string data.

opts.t: Compile error: string cannot convert to string_view
opts.why: std::string implicitly converts to std::string_view, which is exactly why this danger is subtle.

why: std::string_view stores a pointer into the string's buffer. When s is destroyed on function return, the view's pointer dangles. Using the returned view is UB. Return std::string by value or ensure the lifetime of the backing string outlives the view.
fj: "string_view is like a smart string — it manages its own memory."
fs: "string_view is a non-owning reference; never return one pointing at a local std::string."

q: What is wrong with this lambda capture?

opts.t: Dangling reference — x is gone after make() returns
Safe — lambdas extend the lifetime of captured refs
opts.why: Lambdas do not extend lifetime. Capture by reference only keeps a reference, not ownership.

opts.t: Compile error: cannot capture local by reference in lambda
opts.why: Capturing by reference is perfectly legal C++; the danger is purely runtime UB.

why: x is a local variable in make(). Capturing it by reference stores a pointer to x's stack slot. When make() returns, x is destroyed and the lambda holds a dangling reference. Capturing by value ([x]) copies x into the closure, making the returned lambda safe.
fj: "Capturing by reference is safer because you get the latest value."
fs: "Capture by reference only when the lambda's lifetime is strictly within the captured variable's scope."

q: What is wrong with calling a virtual function here?

opts.t: Base::init() is called, not Derived::init()
Derived::init() is called via virtual dispatch
opts.why: During Base::Base(), the Derived portion hasn't been constructed yet. Virtual dispatch resolves to the base version.

opts.t: Undefined behaviour — vtable not yet set up
opts.why: It's well-defined: C++ specifies that during Base construction, the runtime type is Base, so Base::init() is called. It's a design flaw, not UB.

why: During the execution of Base's constructor, the dynamic type of the object is Base, not Derived. Virtual function calls inside constructors (and destructors) dispatch to the class being constructed, not the final overrider. Never call virtual functions in constructors/destructors if you expect derived-class overrides.
fj: "Virtual dispatch always resolves to the most-derived override."
fs: "Virtual calls in constructors/destructors dispatch to the current construction level — avoid them or use a factory pattern."

q: Two threads share a flag. Is this safe?

opts.t: Data race UB — use std::atomic<bool>
Safe — bool reads/writes are atomic on x86
opts.why: Hardware atomicity doesn't satisfy the C++ memory model. The compiler can hoist the load out of the loop or reorder with do_work().

opts.t: Safe if producer runs before consumer starts
opts.why: Without synchronisation, the compiler or CPU may still reorder or cache the value.

why: Concurrent read/write to a non-atomic variable is a data race, which is undefined behaviour in the C++ memory model. The compiler is allowed to cache ready in a register, making the consumer loop infinite. Use std::atomic<bool> ready{false} with appropriate memory_order (at minimum memory_order_acquire/release).
fj: "A plain bool flag is fine for thread signalling on modern CPUs."
fs: "Use std::atomic or a mutex for any shared mutable state — the C++ memory model requires it."

q: Both TUs include this inline function. What is the ODR issue?

opts.t: If DEFAULT_THRESHOLD differs between TUs, ODR is violated
inline guarantees a single definition — no ODR risk
opts.why: inline means 'multiple identical definitions allowed'. If a macro makes the bodies differ between TUs, ODR is violated even with inline.

opts.t: ODR only applies to classes, not functions
opts.why: ODR applies to all entities with external linkage defined in multiple translation units.

why: The One Definition Rule requires that all definitions of the same inline function across TUs are token-for-token identical. If DEFAULT_THRESHOLD is a macro defined differently in different TUs (e.g., via conflicting headers or compiler flags), the function bodies differ — ODR violation, silent UB. Replace with a named constant in a shared header.
fj: "inline avoids ODR issues by merging definitions at link time."
fs: "ODR requires identical token sequences across all TUs — macros that vary per TU silently violate it."

q: What is the static initialization order fiasco here?

opts.t: g_log may init before g_cfg — UB/crash
Safe — linker orders TUs deterministically
opts.why: C++ does not define the initialization order of globals across translation units.

opts.t: Safe because cfg() is a function call, not a direct reference
opts.why: cfg() returns g_cfg which may not yet be constructed when g_log's constructor runs.

why: The order of initialization of non-local static objects across translation units is unspecified. g_log in a.cpp calls cfg() which returns g_cfg from b.cpp; if b.cpp's statics haven't been initialized yet, g_cfg is accessed in an unconstructed state — UB. Fix: use the Meyers singleton (construct-on-first-use) for cfg().
fj: "Global objects are initialized before main(), so ordering doesn't matter."
fs: "Cross-TU static init order is undefined; use construct-on-first-use (local static) to enforce ordering."

q: A compiler optimizes away a bounds check. Why?

opts.t: Signed overflow is UB; compiler assumes i+1 never wraps
The guard i+1 > INT_MAX catches all overflow cases
opts.why: Signed integer overflow is UB in C++. The compiler assumes it never happens and may delete the guard as dead code.

opts.t: The guard is correct; UB only affects unsigned types
opts.why: It is the opposite: unsigned overflow is defined (wraps); signed overflow is undefined behaviour.

why: Signed integer overflow is UB in C++. Because i is int, the compiler assumes i+1 never wraps (overflow would be UB). It therefore proves the condition i+1 > INT_MAX is always false and eliminates it as dead code — the guard vanishes. Use unsigned or __builtin_add_overflow() / LLVM's checked arithmetic.
fj: "Overflow guards with signed ints work on any real hardware."
fs: "Compilers exploit signed-overflow UB for optimization; use unsigned arithmetic or checked builtins for overflow detection."

q: This shared-library header exposes a std::vector. What is the ABI hazard?

opts.t: STL layout differs across compilers/stdlib versions, breaking callers
No issue — std types have a stable ABI across all toolchains
opts.why: The C++ standard does not mandate a stable ABI for std::vector or std::string. libc++/libstdc++ layouts differ.

opts.t: Only a problem with DLL exports on Windows
opts.why: This affects any shared library boundary where caller and callee may be compiled with different toolchains or stdlib versions.

why: std::vector<std::string>'s internal layout (pointer size, SSO fields for string) is implementation-defined and changes across stdlib versions and compilers. A caller compiled with libstdc++ 11 and a library compiled with libstdc++ 12 may use different layouts for the same type, causing silent heap corruption. Public APIs crossing ABI boundaries should use C types, opaque handles, or a versioned ABI-stable struct.
fj: "std::vector is standard — it has the same layout everywhere."
fs: "Never expose std container types across shared-library boundaries; use C-compatible structs or versioned stable types."

q: This singleton is used across many services. What is the production blast radius?

opts.t: Mutable global state couples all callers, making testing and reload impossible
Fine — Meyers singleton is thread-safe in C++11
opts.why: Thread-safe construction is only part of the story. Shared mutable state, inability to reset between tests, and reload coupling are the real prod risks.

opts.t: The only risk is the static init order fiasco
opts.why: Meyers singleton already solves init order via construct-on-first-use. The systemic risks are architectural, not construction order.

why: A mutable global singleton couples every service that includes config.h: (1) unit tests cannot inject a test config without global side-effects; (2) hot-reload or multi-tenant isolation requires locking the global and reasoning about thread-safety for all mutations; (3) any change to Config's internal layout forces a rebuild of every TU. Prefer dependency injection passing a Config reference through constructors or function parameters.
fj: "Singletons are the standard pattern for shared configuration in C++."
fs: "Singletons in a header included org-wide spread coupling; inject Config via constructor params for testability and hot-reload safety."

q: A plugin shared library links against a different allocator than the host. What breaks?

opts.t: Host calling delete on plugin-allocated memory is UB
Fine — the allocator is an implementation detail hidden by the OS
opts.why: new/delete are not virtualized through the OS. Each binary has its own allocator; mixing them corrupts the heap.

opts.t: Only a problem if std::string uses custom operator new
opts.why: std::string's heap nodes are allocated by whichever allocator was linked into the plugin; freeing them via the host's allocator is always wrong.

why: If the plugin links jemalloc and the host uses system malloc, memory allocated by new inside the plugin must be freed by the plugin's delete. A host calling delete on a plugin-returned pointer passes a jemalloc address to system malloc's free(), corrupting the heap. Public APIs crossing DSO boundaries must either own allocation/deallocation entirely on one side (provide a matching freeStr), or use a C ABI with explicit free functions, or agree on a single allocator at link time.
fj: "malloc and jemalloc are compatible — they both manage the same heap."
fs: "Always allocate and deallocate on the same side of a shared-library boundary, or document that callers must use the provided free function."

q: Library header declares noexcept; callers rely on it. What policy mismatch risk exists?
lines: // now may throw on bad input

opts.t: Removing noexcept in .cpp while header says noexcept calls std::terminate if it throws
Callers simply catch the exception as normal
opts.why: When a noexcept function throws, the runtime calls std::terminate immediately — the exception cannot propagate.

opts.t: ABI break only if the function signature changes
opts.why: noexcept is part of the function type in C++17+. Changing it in one TU while another TU sees the noexcept declaration is an ODR violation and can cause std::terminate.

why: noexcept is a contract: if a noexcept function propagates an exception, std::terminate is called. If the implementation is updated to throw without updating the header's noexcept spec, every caller that links against the new implementation will terminate on exception instead of catching it. In C++17 noexcept is part of the function type, so the mismatch is also an ODR violation. Always version the header alongside any exception-spec change and communicate it as a breaking change.
fj: "The noexcept in the header is just a hint — callers can still catch exceptions."
fs: "noexcept is a binding contract; removing it from an implementation while the header still declares it silently replaces exception handling with std::terminate at every call site."
